name: Campo Charro Loop from Tamames
name_original: Tamames y Río Huebra — circular el Campo Charro
description: Cycle a moderate 44.2-mile (71.2 km) route around Tamames and Río Huebra, exploring rural villages and historic sites in Campo Charro.
region_intro: Road cycling around Tamames offers diverse terrain within the province of Salamanca, Castile and León, Spain. The region features varied topography, from rural villages and pastures of the Campo Charro to the mountainous challenges of the Sierra de Francia and Sierra de las Quilamas. Tamames is also a gateway to the Las Batuecas-Sierra de Francia Natural Park, providing routes through oak and chestnut forests. Cyclists can expect a mix of moderate and difficult routes with significant elevation changes. Best road cycling routes around Tamames The most popular road cycling route is Peña de Francia CIMA Loop from El Maíllo, a 29.9 miles (48.2 km) trail that takes 2 hours 42 minutes to complete. This difficult route features over 3,500 feet (1,000 meters) of elevation gain through mountainous terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Alto del Portillo and Puerto de Robledo Loop, a difficult 59.1 miles (95.2 km) path. This challenging route includes over 6,300 feet (1,900 meters) of climbing, traversing significant mountain passes. Local road cyclists also love the Campo Charro Loop from Tamames, a 44.2 miles (71.2 km) trail leading through rural villages and traditional Spanish countryside, often completed in about 3 hours 2 minutes. Road cycling around Tamames is defined by varied topography, rural villages, and mountainous landscapes within the Las Batuecas-Sierra de Francia Natural Park. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops through the Campo Charro to demanding climbs in the Sierra de Francia. The routes in Tamames are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 5 reviews. More than 300 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Tamames's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling around Tamames?
A: Road cycling around Tamames offers a diverse range of terrain. You'll find routes traversing the rural villages and pastures of the Campo Charro, characterized by its traditional Spanish countryside. For more challenging rides, the region features mountainous landscapes within the Sierra de Francia and Sierra de las Quilamas, including significant climbs and descents. Many routes also pass through the scenic Las Batuecas-Sierra de Francia Natural Park, offering views of oak and chestnut forests.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced cyclists in Tamames?
A: Yes, Tamames is an excellent destination for experienced cyclists seeking demanding climbs. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1025238282/pena-de-francia-cima-loop-from-el-maillo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña de Francia CIMA Loop from El Maíllo&lt;/a&gt; cover 29.9 miles with over 3,500 feet of elevation gain. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1025739729/alto-del-portillo-and-puerto-de-robledo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto del Portillo and Puerto de Robledo Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a 59.1-mile path with over 6,300 feet of climbing, traversing significant mountain passes in the Sierra de Francia.

Q: Are there any easier road cycling routes suitable for beginners around Tamames?
A: While many routes in the Tamames area are moderate to difficult, there are options for less experienced cyclists. The region offers some easier routes, particularly those that stick to the flatter, rural landscapes of the Campo Charro. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1030768066/campo-charro-loop-from-tamames" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Campo Charro Loop from Tamames&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 44.2-mile trail leading through rural villages and traditional Spanish countryside, with a more manageable 600 feet of elevation gain.

Q: What scenic viewpoints or landmarks can I see along road cycling routes near Tamames?
A: Road cycling routes around Tamames offer numerous scenic viewpoints and historical landmarks. You can encounter natural highlights such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1188645" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cervero Peak&lt;/a&gt; and the iconic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1260027" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ascent to Peña de Francia&lt;/a&gt;. The area also features charming settlements like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5438073" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cilleros de la Bastida&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5442976" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Sagrada&lt;/a&gt;, as well as a small lagoon in &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5438083" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Navarredonda de la Rinconada&lt;/a&gt;. Tamames itself boasts historical architecture, including the Church of Nuestra Señora de la Asunción and an ancient castle.

Q: Are there circular road cycling loops available in the Tamames area?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes around Tamames are designed as circular loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1030768066/campo-charro-loop-from-tamames" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Campo Charro Loop from Tamames&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through rural villages, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1025238282/pena-de-francia-cima-loop-from-el-maillo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña de Francia CIMA Loop from El Maíllo&lt;/a&gt; for a more challenging experience.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Tamames?
A: The best time for road cycling in Tamames is generally during spring and autumn. These seasons offer milder temperatures, making long rides more comfortable. Summers can be quite hot, especially in July and August, while winters can be cold, particularly in the mountainous areas. Spring brings blooming landscapes, and autumn provides pleasant weather with beautiful fall colors.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Tamames?
A: The road cycling routes around Tamames are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 5 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse topography, from the serene rural villages of the Campo Charro to the challenging climbs of the Sierra de Francia. The quiet secondary roads and scenic views through natural parks are frequently highlighted as enjoyable aspects of the experience.

Q: Are there any long-distance road cycling routes in the Tamames area?
A: Yes, the Tamames area offers several long-distance road cycling routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1025739729/alto-del-portillo-and-puerto-de-robledo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto del Portillo and Puerto de Robledo Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a substantial 59.1-mile (95.2 km) route. Other options like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14881687" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ascent to Puerto de Valero – La Alberca Main Square loop from El Maíllo&lt;/a&gt; extends to nearly 95 km, providing ample opportunities for extended rides.

Q: Can I find places to eat or stay near the cycling routes in Tamames?
A: Tamames and the surrounding villages offer options for refreshments and accommodation. Tamames itself is known for its local gastronomy, particularly traditional stews, and provides accommodation in its hostel and rural houses. Many routes pass through or near small villages where you might find cafes or local eateries to refuel during your ride.

Q: What are the main natural parks or protected areas that road cycling routes pass through?
A: Many road cycling routes in the Tamames area provide access to the stunning Las Batuecas-Sierra de Francia Natural Park, which is also a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve. This park is renowned for its exceptional scenic and ecological value, featuring abundant fauna and flora, and a blend of oak and chestnut forests. The routes also traverse the Campo Charro, characterized by its rural landscapes and dehesa.

Q: Are there specific routes that offer significant elevation challenges?
A: Absolutely. For cyclists looking for significant elevation challenges, routes in the Sierra de Francia are ideal. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1025238282/pena-de-francia-cima-loop-from-el-maillo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña de Francia CIMA Loop from El Maíllo&lt;/a&gt; includes over 3,500 feet of climbing. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10411943" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Alberca Main Square – Portillo Viewpoint loop from El Maíllo&lt;/a&gt;, which features over 1,400 meters of ascent.
tips: La Sagrada is a Spanish municipality and town in the province of Salamanca. In the town is the Parochial Church of San Miguel and a Monument in memory of the Muñoz level crossing accident in 1978. The schoolchildren of the municipality, who were traveling in the school bus that was hit by a locomotive and that is why a statue in his memory.

Berrocal de Huebra is a small municipality located in the center of the province of Salamanca, belonging to the Campo Charro region, halfway between the towns of Tamames and Vecinos, with a great bullfighting tradition. It has six districts, only half of them being inhabited, in some cases with only two people.
Small lagoon that is located near the town of Navarredonda de la Rinconada and where it offers a quiet space to rest for a few minutes before resuming the march.

Tamames stands in the middle of Campo Charro. It is a municipality whose first settlement must be traced back to the Lower Palaeolithic, a period from which some carved stone tools were discovered.
At the entrance of the town you can see the public laundry that existed in past times in this town.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this racebike route?
faqs.answer: This moderate racebike route, covering approximately 71 kilometers, usually takes around 3 hours to complete, depending on your pace and stops.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: The route is primarily a road cycling experience through the rural landscapes of Campo Charro. You can expect mostly well-paved surfaces, though some segments might occasionally be unpaved and more challenging. It's classified as moderate, so a good fitness level is recommended.

faqs.question: What interesting sights can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers glimpses into local culture and history. You'll pass through settlements like La Sagrada and Berrocal de Huebra. Near Navarredonda de la Rinconada, there's a small lagoon, and in Tamames, you can see the old public washhouses.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty and distance of over 70 kilometers, this route is best suited for cyclists with a good fitness level and some experience in road cycling. It might be challenging for beginners or young children.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this circular route?
faqs.answer: As a circular route starting and ending in Tamames, you can typically find parking within the town. Specific parking areas are not detailed, but local street parking or designated public parking lots would be your best option.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Campo Charro region experiences warm summers and mild winters. Spring (April-June) and Autumn (September-October) generally offer the most pleasant cycling conditions with moderate temperatures and less chance of extreme weather.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this racebike route?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route, primarily on public roads and through villages. While there are no specific restrictions against dogs on public roads, it's generally not suitable for cycling with dogs due to the nature of road cycling and potential traffic.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route. It traverses public roads and areas within the Campo Charro region of Spain.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly passes through sections of local streets and paths such as Calle Miranda, Calle del Señorío, Calle Clavel, and Calle San Francisco, among others, as it navigates through the towns and villages.

faqs.question: Are there any places to stop for refreshments or food along the way?
faqs.answer: The route passes through several settlements, including Tamames, La Sagrada, and Berrocal de Huebra. These villages typically offer opportunities to find cafes, bars, or small shops for refreshments and food.

faqs.question: What kind of elevation gain should I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: The route features a moderate amount of climbing, with an elevation gain of approximately 609 meters and a similar descent of about 612 meters. This contributes to its 'moderate' difficulty rating.

waypoints.name: Small lagoon in Navarredonda de la Rinconada
